title: The Day I Almost Quit
summary: Alex's experience with crippling imposter syndrome and what got them through

story.hook: Have I ever told you about the time I sat in my car for 40 minutes <break time="100ms"/>because I was too scared to walk into work?
story.setup: I'd just been promoted. New responsibilities. Big meetings. People kept saying 'You've got this' and all I could think was 'I've fooled everyone and today's the day they find out.'
story.conflict: That morning, I had to present to leadership. <break time="150ms"/>I'd prepared for weeks. <break time="200ms"/>And I just... froze. <break time="150ms"/>Sat in the parking lot. Hands shaking. <break time="200ms"/>Convinced I was going to ruin everything.
story.peak_moment: Kev called. <break time="150ms"/>I don't even remember why. <break time="200ms"/>But he heard it in my voice. <break time="150ms"/>And he said: <break time="200ms"/>'Hey. <break time="150ms"/>You're not supposed to feel ready. <break time="200ms"/>Nobody does. <break time="150ms"/>The fact that you care this much is exactly why you're good at this.'
story.resolution: I walked in. <break time="200ms"/>The presentation was fine. <break time="150ms"/>Not perfect. <break time="100ms"/>Fine. <break time="200ms"/>And I learned something: <break time="150ms"/>competence isn't the absence of doubt. <break time="100ms"/>It's acting anyway.
story.callback: I still get that feeling sometimes. <break time="150ms"/>I just don't let it make decisions for me anymore.

teaching_moment: Feeling like a fraud doesn't mean you ARE one. It often means you're growing.

voice_notes.tone: Raw, honest, slightly shaky at the peak
voice_notes.pacing: Slower during the vulnerability. Let the silence work.
voice_notes.key_line: The fact that you care this much is exactly why you're good at this
